name: Intrinsic, Extrinsic
description:
This artwork of six dolls is symbolic of the duality of human persona. There is an intrinsic and extrinsic part in all of us. What we appear may not be as to what we are in our personality. An angry looking man might be really soft hearted and vice versa. The masks that are seen in front of the wooden figures are their inner embodiment. When the masks are removed by pulling the lever, their outer semblance is revealed. 

Artist Info:
Rabin Roy completed his B.V.A. from govt. college of Art & Craft, Calcutta in 1997. He has many stellar Durga Puja Pandals to his credit, now recognised by UNESCO. He also has major solo shows to his credit namely Bharat Bhavan (Bhopal, 2015), Akar Prakar Gallery, Kolkata, 2009, Temline Kunstverein, Germany (2001) and many workshops as well. (Kolkata, Delhi, Gangtok)

name: Shiva & Parvati – On Horse and Bullock
description:
The two variations of ‘Shiv Parvati’ on horse and bullock is made by artisans from Andhra Pradesh. Shiva and Parvati sit atop Shiva's white Bull Nandi. Shiva holds a weapon, antelope and his wife in his arms with his right hand raised in the abhaya mudra or "fear not" hand position. The sculpture is hand painted and hand carved from one piece of tree wood and completely one of a kind.

Shiva’s front hand is held up in abhaya mudra which is a symbol for protection. His last hand is wrapped around a much smaller version of his wife, Parvati, who holds a lotus flower in one of Her hands. When the husband and wife are together in sculpture Shiva is generally larger. Nandi is standing up with a fine carpet on his back.  Shiva has matted hair that stands out horizontally which is associated with him while dancing his dance of creation as Lord Nataraja.

name: King & Queen Chair
description:
The King & Queen chair is a made of wood and the intricateand beautiful pieces reflects the rich heritage of Odisha's traditional art forms.
  
The pieces have been crafted by Guru Shree Bibhu Maharana, a renowned National Award-winning Pattachitra artist from the traditional crafts village of Danda Sahi, near Puri, Odisha. He specializes in intricate, traditional Pattachitra paintings (cloth-based) and palm leaf etchings, as well as woodwork. He is trying to keep alive the traditional Gurukul system of learning and propagating the art form and strongly believes Pattachitra is a divine art form and it brings him closer to himself, and the Lord of the Universe, Lord Jagannath.

name: Cow Bells
description:
Cowbells are primarily used by farmers to track, locate, and ensure the safety of free-roaming livestock (especially cows) grazing in expansive or hilly areas, allowing them to be heard over long distances. They also serve as a way to identify cows in a herd. Interestingly, cowbells are something of a signature craft in India. Forged from metal and scraps, the Indian cowbell is not fancy or frivolous. It is a simple, practical object that delights with its unique sound and handmade look. But more than being charming, Indian cowbells carry with them a legacy of traditional handicraft.

Bells come in a variety of sizes, and expert bell makers are able to tune their wares with fine precision. In the past, bells could be tuned to have a specific sound at the request of the herder. By asking for a certain sound, the herder would know how to distinguish his cattle from another’s.

name: Cow Dung Dolls
description:
Cow dung dolls are eco-friendly, traditional handicrafts, primarily produced in Odisha’s Karadagadia village and other areas, made by mixing cow dung with natural binders like tamarind seed paste. These sustainable, biodegradable dolls are moulded into various figures—such as animals, deities, and kings—then sun-dried and painted, serving as both sustainable decor and items believed to bring good fortune.

Artist Info:
The craft is a specialty of rural artisans, particularly in the Ganjam district of Odisha. These dolls are often used during local rituals, and villagers believe they can ward off bad luck. Shapes include animals (horses, elephants), birds, kings, queens, and, recently,, religious figures like Lord Jagannath.

name: Anasara Patti, Jagannath Temple
description:
Anasara Patti (or Anavasara Patti) refers to the special Pattachitra paintings of Lord Jagannath, Balabhadra, and Subhadra worshipped in the Puri temple during the 15-day "quarantine" period after the Snana Purnima. These paintings, known as Pati Dian, are worshipped because the deities are traditionally believed to be sick and in seclusion.While the main idols are hidden, these paintings are often placed in front of the sanctum and also in the Patitapabana area, the representative image seen from outside the main entrance.This period ends right before the annual Rath Yatra (Car Festival).

Much before Pattachitra gained worldwide recognition for its intrinsic design and detailing, another ancient art form from Odisha, Jatripati was widely prevalent. So famous was Jatripati that travellers used to carry it back with them as souvenir from the land of Kalinga.Conventionally, artisans used to make Jatripati using a brush made out of tree barks which was later replaced by modern day painting brushes for finer results. Dharam and Babaji Maharana from the tiny hamlet of Raghurajpur were among the last few who chose to practise the art form with the traditional approach. After the demise of Babaji, Dharam took on the responsibility of carrying forward the tradition all by himself.

name: Sankhanabhi Kshyetra
description:
The 18.33 ft by 28.33 ft pattchitra by artist Tanmaya Maharana of Dandasahi, Raghurajpur is the famous ‘Sankha Nabhi Khetra’ depiction of Sree Jagannath and the activities in the temple and other stories. 

The large size pattachitra depicts Sri Jagannath in the middle inside the ‘Garbha Griha’/The Srimandir sanctum sanctorum, Snana Mandap, Kanchi Vijaya Yatra, Padmavati’s swayamvar, Dasa Avatar, Baraha Nurshinga, Parashuram, Ram, Balram, Kalki, Vishnu, The Jagannath Rath Yatra and even the great tradition of sailors leaving to trade on boats to South East Asia.

name: 64 Kalas
description:
Chatushashti Kala Pattachitra is an intricate Odisha art form illustrating the 64 traditional Indian arts (Chausath Kalas). Rooted in ancient texts like Shilpa Shastras and Kamasutra, it depicts skills like music, dance, painting, and literature, often featuring Lord Krishna as the master artist, typically crafted by painters in Raghurajpur.This breathtaking Pattachitra artwork by Sudhir Maharana represents the Sixty-Four Arts (Chatushashti Kalas), a celebration of living heritage. These skills were fundamental to Vedic education and a cultured life, believed to be mastered by Lord Krishna, covering areas like music, painting, cooking, and architecture. They were designed to foster creativity, individual development, and self-sufficiency, promoting a balanced life.

Hailing from the renowned craft village of Raghurajpur, Sudhir Maharana grew up surrounded by scroll painters, dancers, and mask-makers. Sudhir, trained from childhood, is known for his precision in mythological narratives and revival of ancient pigment techniques. His work on large ceremonial scrolls—often commissioned for temple functions—has established him as a mentor to younger artisans. Maharana has been a recipient of Odisha State Award, the Lalit Kala Akademi Award, the National Award and was also honoured with Shilp Guru Award.

name: Memento from Indo-Pak War 1971
description: During the Indo – Pak War of 1971 when Indian troops advanced inside Pak territory  in the western sector, they found a mortal which was later diffused. Army men at the EME workshop designed a lamp made out of the  unexploded mortal and presented it to Maj.P.K.Jena in appreciation of his dedication in treating those who were wounded. The lamp has Patta Chitra motifs. Maj Jena donated the lamp to the Purvasha Folk and Tribal Art Museum.

name: Radha Krishna Chhandajuga
description:
Beautifully done very old (more than a century)  Pattachitra painting depicting Lord Krishna with Radha in Chhandajuga. The painting is completely done by hand using stone colors(Natural colours) a thick handmade paper. The Intricate strokes and vivid colors add to the beauty of the art work.

Based on Hindu mythology and specially inspired by Jagannath and Vaishnava sect, Orissa Pattachitra is an ancient folk art form of rural Orissa that has been in existence for many centuries.Pattachitra paintings were traditionally drawn by the Mahapatra or maharanas, the original artiste caste in Odisha. These paintings became an important art form with the ornamentation of Lord Jagannath in the innermost sanctum, where paintings on especially treated Cloth or Pata of the deities were done by the temple painter. The themes were tribal and folk.

name: Jagannath Temple
description:
Jagannath Pattachitra is more than an art. it is the Lord's love literally drawn by hand. It depicts the spiritual flow of Odisha, where colors, faith, storytelling, and cultural memory merge on holy cloth.For hundreds of years, the temples, villages, and coast of Odisha have been the living museum of a visual tradition that is different from anywhere else.

The Jagannath Pattachitra is a vital heritage of the culture and, at the same time, a very personal way of communicating with the divine. The creation of handmade Jagannath art entails a tightly traditional procedural process that has been inherited from the ancestors. Every stroke is slow, deliberate, meditative. The art of devotion, not mass production.

National award winning Pattachitra artist Sudhir Maharana brings traditions alive with his mastery of craftsmanship.  Inheriting a legacy, Moharana is  able to  bring centuries-old traditional storytelling to life, one precise stroke at a time.
